quotes.verbatim_quote:
The down-up stroke
         This is the most common stroke on the guitar.
         Use this exercise to control your accent on the downbeats.
         Apply to all strings.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes the default picking stroke and its purpose: accenting downbeats across all strings.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Practice keeping your wrist straight but not stiff. Remember: the movement is from the elbow.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Specifies the load-bearing mechanical rule for right-hand motion that governs every picking exercise in the book.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The up-down stroke

     Use this exercise to control your accent on the downbeats but with an up-stroke.
     Apply to all strings.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Introduces the inverted-accent variant, training the upstroke to carry the downbeat accent.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Do not touch the guitar with the fingers of the right hand.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the canonical right-hand-isolation rule that prevents anchoring and constrains picking technique throughout the method.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Mixed strokes
      This exercise is designed to alternate the accents with a down-stroke then an up-stroke.
      Apply to all strings.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines accent alternation between down and up strokes, the synthesis of exercises 1 and 2.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Do not touch the pick guard or strings with the fingers of the right hand.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Extends the no-anchor rule to the pick guard, reinforcing free-floating right-hand posture.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
In between stroke

           This is the hardest stroke to master. It involves going between two adjacent strings.
           This is a critical part of your right hand technique. Take your time with this one.
           Apply to any two adjacent strings using the double-stop of your choice. Do not let notes ring!
quotes.why_load_bearing: Names the hardest stroke and prescribes its application: adjacent-string double-stops with no sustain, central to right-hand mastery.
